title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 03

questions.prompt: Originalized royal chronicle, Ethiopian highlands, c. 1320: “The king restored the church lands and ordered the nobles to protect the plow villages. Priests blessed his line as descended from ancient kings, and the court sent judges to settle disputes among the mountain districts.” What was the most likely purpose of this chronicle?

choices.A: To encourage Ethiopia to abandon Christianity and adopt steppe traditions
choices.B: To promote merchant control over the monarchy
choices.C: To legitimize royal authority through religion, lineage, and protection of agrarian society
choices.D: To argue that Ethiopian rulers had no authority outside coastal ports

questions.correctAnswerText: To legitimize royal authority through religion, lineage, and protection of agrarian society
questions.explanation: The chronicle links the king to church lands, ancient lineage, judges, nobles, and village protection.

questions.prompt: Originalized royal chronicle, Ethiopian highlands, c. 1320: “The king restored the church lands and ordered the nobles to protect the plow villages. Priests blessed his line as descended from ancient kings, and the court sent judges to settle disputes among the mountain districts.” Which broader context best explains the chronicle’s emphasis on priests and church lands?

choices.A: Christianity remained an important source of political and cultural legitimacy in Ethiopia.
choices.B: Buddhism replaced Christianity as the official religion of the Ethiopian court.
choices.C: Ethiopian rulers rejected written religious traditions after 1200.
choices.D: The Ethiopian state depended entirely on Atlantic plantation exports.

questions.correctAnswerText: Christianity remained an important source of political and cultural legitimacy in Ethiopia.
questions.explanation: The reference to priests, church lands, and sacred royal lineage reflects Ethiopia’s Christian political culture.

questions.prompt: Originalized royal chronicle, Ethiopian highlands, c. 1320: “The king restored the church lands and ordered the nobles to protect the plow villages. Priests blessed his line as descended from ancient kings, and the court sent judges to settle disputes among the mountain districts.” Which phrase best supports the claim that royal power was linked to social order?

choices.A: “Priests blessed his line”
choices.B: “Ancient kings”
choices.C: “Ethiopian highlands”
choices.D: “Sent judges to settle disputes among the mountain districts”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Sent judges to settle disputes among the mountain districts”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects royal authority to maintaining order across communities.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “The stone walls of Great Zimbabwe were built without mortar, enclosed elite spaces, and stood near areas where imported glass beads, local cattle wealth, and gold-working evidence have been found.” Which interpretation is best supported by this description?

choices.A: Great Zimbabwe was an isolated village with no social hierarchy.
choices.B: Monumental architecture and luxury goods reflected elite power and regional exchange.
choices.C: Great Zimbabwe’s rulers depended on gunpowder weapons to build authority.
choices.D: The city was a European colonial settlement founded after 1750.

questions.correctAnswerText: Monumental architecture and luxury goods reflected elite power and regional exchange.
questions.explanation: Elite enclosures, stone walls, imported beads, cattle wealth, and gold-working all suggest hierarchy and exchange.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “The stone walls of Great Zimbabwe were built without mortar, enclosed elite spaces, and stood near areas where imported glass beads, local cattle wealth, and gold-working evidence have been found.” The description most directly reflects which development in southern Africa?

choices.A: The decline of all trade connections between inland and coastal regions
choices.B: The replacement of cattle wealth by industrial wage labor
choices.C: The rise of a powerful inland state connected to regional commerce
choices.D: The creation of a plantation colony by European settlers

questions.correctAnswerText: The rise of a powerful inland state connected to regional commerce
questions.explanation: Great Zimbabwe was an inland state whose wealth was connected to cattle, gold, and regional trade.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “The stone walls of Great Zimbabwe were built without mortar, enclosed elite spaces, and stood near areas where imported glass beads, local cattle wealth, and gold-working evidence have been found.” Which broader exchange context best explains the presence of imported glass beads?

choices.A: Inland southern African communities had connections to Indian Ocean commercial networks through coastal intermediaries.
choices.B: The Atlantic slave trade supplied Great Zimbabwe with manufactured goods after 1600.
choices.C: European railroads carried glass beads into the interior before 1200.
choices.D: Great Zimbabwe’s elites banned all foreign goods from entering their territory.

questions.correctAnswerText: Inland southern African communities had connections to Indian Ocean commercial networks through coastal intermediaries.
questions.explanation: Imported glass beads suggest indirect links between inland elites and coastal Indian Ocean trade.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation: “The stone walls of Great Zimbabwe were built without mortar, enclosed elite spaces, and stood near areas where imported glass beads, local cattle wealth, and gold-working evidence have been found.” Which evidence best supports the claim that social hierarchy existed at Great Zimbabwe?

choices.A: The use of unmortared stone
choices.B: The location in southern Africa
choices.C: The presence of cattle wealth
choices.D: The enclosed elite spaces

questions.correctAnswerText: The enclosed elite spaces
questions.explanation: Separate elite spaces are direct evidence of social hierarchy.

questions.prompt: Described map and visual source: “A map of Central and East Asia c. 800–1400 shows Buddhist cave sites near Dunhuang, Turfan, and Kucha along caravan corridors. A described cave mural shows donors wearing Central Asian clothing beside painted Buddhist figures.” Which development is best reflected by the map and mural?

choices.A: Religious and artistic traditions spread through Silk Road exchange.
choices.B: Buddhism originated in Central Asia after 1400.
choices.C: Caravan routes prevented the movement of religious ideas.
choices.D: East Asian religions disappeared because of merchant travel.

questions.correctAnswerText: Religious and artistic traditions spread through Silk Road exchange.
questions.explanation: Buddhist cave sites along caravan corridors show religious and artistic diffusion through trade networks.

questions.prompt: Described map and visual source: “A map of Central and East Asia c. 800–1400 shows Buddhist cave sites near Dunhuang, Turfan, and Kucha along caravan corridors. A described cave mural shows donors wearing Central Asian clothing beside painted Buddhist figures.” Which feature most directly shows cultural blending?

choices.A: The use of a map
choices.B: The cave location
choices.C: The dates c. 800–1400
choices.D: Central Asian donors shown beside Buddhist figures

questions.correctAnswerText: Central Asian donors shown beside Buddhist figures
questions.explanation: Central Asian donors represented in Buddhist art show cultural blending along the Silk Roads.

questions.prompt: Described map and visual source: “A map of Central and East Asia c. 800–1400 shows Buddhist cave sites near Dunhuang, Turfan, and Kucha along caravan corridors. A described cave mural shows donors wearing Central Asian clothing beside painted Buddhist figures.” Which claim is best supported by the location of the cave sites?

choices.A: Buddhist sites were built only in coastal port cities.
choices.B: Cultural exchange often followed routes shaped by oasis geography and caravan travel.
choices.C: Central Asian deserts prevented all long-distance interaction.
choices.D: Religious art had no relationship to trade routes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cultural exchange often followed routes shaped by oasis geography and caravan travel.
questions.explanation: Cave sites near oasis routes indicate that geography shaped cultural and religious exchange.

questions.prompt: Originalized Asante court statement, c. 1720: “The golden stool is the soul of the people. Let chiefs bring cases to Kumasi, let roads be cleared for messengers, and let gold weights be tested so that trade and tribute may be just.” Which broader context best explains this statement?

choices.A: The disappearance of centralized states in West Africa after 1450
choices.B: The rise of constitutional monarchy in industrial Europe
choices.C: The growth of a West African state that combined sacred kingship, administration, and commerce
choices.D: The creation of an overseas settler colony in southern Africa

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of a West African state that combined sacred kingship, administration, and commerce
questions.explanation: The golden stool, chiefs, Kumasi, messengers, gold weights, trade, and tribute show sacred and administrative state power.

questions.prompt: Originalized Asante court statement, c. 1720: “The golden stool is the soul of the people. Let chiefs bring cases to Kumasi, let roads be cleared for messengers, and let gold weights be tested so that trade and tribute may be just.” Which point of view most likely shaped the statement?

choices.A: A European abolitionist’s criticism of plantation slavery
choices.B: A court-centered view emphasizing the sacred and administrative authority of the Asante ruler
choices.C: A Buddhist monk’s description of monastery life
choices.D: A factory owner’s defense of child labor

questions.correctAnswerText: A court-centered view emphasizing the sacred and administrative authority of the Asante ruler
questions.explanation: The statement presents Asante authority from the perspective of the court and ruler.

questions.prompt: Originalized Asante court statement, c. 1720: “The golden stool is the soul of the people. Let chiefs bring cases to Kumasi, let roads be cleared for messengers, and let gold weights be tested so that trade and tribute may be just.” Which phrase most directly supports the claim that the state regulated economic activity?

choices.A: “Soul of the people”
choices.B: “Chiefs bring cases”
choices.C: “Trade and tribute may be just”
choices.D: “The golden stool”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Trade and tribute may be just”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows state concern with regulating commerce and tribute.

questions.prompt: Originalized Asante court statement, c. 1720: “The golden stool is the soul of the people. Let chiefs bring cases to Kumasi, let roads be cleared for messengers, and let gold weights be tested so that trade and tribute may be just.” The reference to roads and messengers most directly reflects which state-building process?

choices.A: The use of communication systems to integrate political authority
choices.B: The replacement of African trade with European railroads
choices.C: The abandonment of inland administration
choices.D: The creation of industrial telegraph networks

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of communication systems to integrate political authority
questions.explanation: Roads and messengers helped connect chiefs and regions to the Asante capital.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ming provincial memorial, c. 1580: “The magistrate reports that households have been registered, granaries inspected, and corvée duties converted into silver payments where local conditions allow. Yet villages complain that clerks hide exemptions and demand extra fees.” What was the most likely purpose of this memorial?

choices.A: To report administrative conditions and abuses to higher officials
choices.B: To invite European companies to govern Chinese provinces
choices.C: To abolish taxation throughout the empire
choices.D: To announce the end of village agriculture

questions.correctAnswerText: To report administrative conditions and abuses to higher officials
questions.explanation: The memorial reports household registration, granaries, tax changes, and clerk abuses to the state.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ming provincial memorial, c. 1580: “The magistrate reports that households have been registered, granaries inspected, and corvée duties converted into silver payments where local conditions allow. Yet villages complain that clerks hide exemptions and demand extra fees.” Which development is most directly reflected by the conversion of labor duties into silver payments?

choices.A: The end of monetized exchange in East Asia
choices.B: The growing importance of silver in taxation and state finance
choices.C: The replacement of agriculture by factory production
choices.D: The disappearance of local officials from imperial governance

questions.correctAnswerText: The growing importance of silver in taxation and state finance
questions.explanation: The conversion of labor duties into silver payments reflects silver’s role in Ming fiscal systems.

questions.prompt: Originalized Ming provincial memorial, c. 1580: “The magistrate reports that households have been registered, granaries inspected, and corvée duties converted into silver payments where local conditions allow. Yet villages complain that clerks hide exemptions and demand extra fees.” Which social tension is most directly suggested by the passage?

choices.A: Conflict between village households and local officials over taxation and labor obligations
choices.B: Conflict between industrial workers and factory owners over wages
choices.C: Conflict between nomadic herders and Buddhist monks over pilgrimage sites
choices.D: Conflict between overseas settlers and Indigenous peoples over plantation land

questions.correctAnswerText: Conflict between village households and local officials over taxation and labor obligations
questions.explanation: Villagers complain about clerks hiding exemptions and demanding extra fees.

questions.prompt:
Table: Potosí silver mining, simplified colonial pattern
Element | Pattern described by officials
Labor | Rotating draft labor from Andean communities
Output | Large quantities of silver sent to colonial treasuries
Supplies | Mercury, mules, food, and cloth brought from other regions
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Potosí was economically isolated from surrounding regions.
choices.B: Silver mining depended only on voluntary wage labor.
choices.C: Mining eliminated the need for colonial administration.
choices.D: American silver extraction connected coerced labor, regional supply networks, and imperial revenue.

questions.correctAnswerText: American silver extraction connected coerced labor, regional supply networks, and imperial revenue.
questions.explanation: The table links draft labor, silver sent to treasuries, and supplies brought from multiple regions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Potosí silver mining, simplified colonial pattern
Element | Pattern described by officials
Labor | Rotating draft labor from Andean communities
Output | Large quantities of silver sent to colonial treasuries
Supplies | Mercury, mules, food, and cloth brought from other regions
Which evidence most directly supports the claim that coercive labor was central to silver production?

choices.A: “Rotating draft labor from Andean communities”
choices.B: “Large quantities of silver”
choices.C: “Food, and cloth”
choices.D: “Colonial treasuries”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Rotating draft labor from Andean communities”
questions.explanation: Rotating draft labor shows coerced labor mobilization for mining.

questions.prompt:
Table: Potosí silver mining, simplified colonial pattern
Element | Pattern described by officials
Labor | Rotating draft labor from Andean communities
Output | Large quantities of silver sent to colonial treasuries
Supplies | Mercury, mules, food, and cloth brought from other regions
Which environmental consequence was most directly associated with mining systems like Potosí?

choices.A: The restoration of forests around mining sites
choices.B: The end of pollution from metal production
choices.C: Landscape disruption and toxic contamination from mining and mercury use
choices.D: The disappearance of urban settlement in mining regions

questions.correctAnswerText: Landscape disruption and toxic contamination from mining and mercury use
questions.explanation: Silver mining and mercury processing damaged landscapes and created toxic pollution.

questions.prompt:
Table: Potosí silver mining, simplified colonial pattern
Element | Pattern described by officials
Labor | Rotating draft labor from Andean communities
Output | Large quantities of silver sent to colonial treasuries
Supplies | Mercury, mules, food, and cloth brought from other regions
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Spanish colonial rule avoided involvement in economic extraction.
choices.B: Colonial states used administrative power to mobilize labor and resources for imperial profit.
choices.C: Silver mining weakened all imperial governments immediately.
choices.D: Mining centers depended entirely on local self-sufficiency.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial states used administrative power to mobilize labor and resources for imperial profit.
questions.explanation: Draft labor, treasuries, and supply networks show colonial administrative extraction.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of a Nahua pictorial annal, central Mexico, sixteenth century: “The painted sequence shows a town before conquest, then riders and armed men arriving, then rows of small burial bundles, and finally local leaders presenting tribute under a new Spanish-style building.” Which change over time is best shown by the sequence?

choices.A: The disappearance of all Indigenous political structures before Europeans arrived
choices.B: The replacement of disease by industrial pollution as the main cause of mortality
choices.C: Conquest brought political disruption, demographic catastrophe, and new tribute arrangements.
choices.D: Spanish rule immediately ended tribute obligations in central Mexico.

questions.correctAnswerText: Conquest brought political disruption, demographic catastrophe, and new tribute arrangements.
questions.explanation: The annal sequence shows conquest, death, and tribute under Spanish colonial rule.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of a Nahua pictorial annal, central Mexico, sixteenth century: “The painted sequence shows a town before conquest, then riders and armed men arriving, then rows of small burial bundles, and finally local leaders presenting tribute under a new Spanish-style building.” The rows of burial bundles most directly reflect which broader process?

choices.A: The spread of epidemic diseases in the Americas after transoceanic contact
choices.B: The growth of industrial cities in Latin America
choices.C: The end of biological exchange after 1450
choices.D: The survival of the Aztec Empire as an independent state

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of epidemic diseases in the Americas after transoceanic contact
questions.explanation: Burial bundles after conquest point to demographic catastrophe from epidemic disease.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source interpretation of a Nahua pictorial annal, central Mexico, sixteenth century: “The painted sequence shows a town before conquest, then riders and armed men arriving, then rows of small burial bundles, and finally local leaders presenting tribute under a new Spanish-style building.” Which statement best evaluates the value of the pictorial annal as evidence?

choices.A: It is useful because it shows how Indigenous artists recorded conquest, disease, and colonial tribute from a local perspective.
choices.B: It is useless because visual sources cannot contain historical information.
choices.C: It proves that Indigenous communities welcomed conquest without resistance.
choices.D: It shows only European opinions about industrialization.

questions.correctAnswerText: It is useful because it shows how Indigenous artists recorded conquest, disease, and colonial tribute from a local perspective.
questions.explanation: The pictorial annal preserves Indigenous visual evidence of colonial transformation.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by an Indian social reformer, c. 1830: “A society cannot call itself enlightened while widows are burned, girls are denied learning, and sacred words are guarded from public reason. We must use both moral law and useful knowledge to reform custom.” Which audience was the letter most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: Mongol commanders organizing relay stations
choices.B: Educated Indians and colonial officials debating social and educational reform
choices.C: Plantation owners defending slavery in the Caribbean
choices.D: Cold War leaders negotiating military alliances

questions.correctAnswerText: Educated Indians and colonial officials debating social and educational reform
questions.explanation: The letter addresses reform of custom, education, and public reason in a colonial Indian reform context.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by an Indian social reformer, c. 1830: “A society cannot call itself enlightened while widows are burned, girls are denied learning, and sacred words are guarded from public reason. We must use both moral law and useful knowledge to reform custom.” Which broader context best explains the ideas expressed in the letter?

choices.A: The spread of reform movements that challenged social customs and promoted education
choices.B: The disappearance of social hierarchy across South Asia before 1750
choices.C: The end of British influence in India before reform debates began
choices.D: The rise of fascist ideology in South Asia during the 1830s

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of reform movements that challenged social customs and promoted education
questions.explanation: The letter fits nineteenth-century reform movements focused on education, gender, and social practices.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by an Indian social reformer, c. 1830: “A society cannot call itself enlightened while widows are burned, girls are denied learning, and sacred words are guarded from public reason. We must use both moral law and useful knowledge to reform custom.” Which statement best evaluates the reformer’s argument?

choices.A: The reformer rejects education as a threat to social improvement.
choices.B: The reformer defends inherited custom as beyond criticism.
choices.C: The reformer combines moral criticism and rational inquiry to justify social reform.
choices.D: The reformer argues that political independence is impossible without industrialization.

questions.correctAnswerText: The reformer combines moral criticism and rational inquiry to justify social reform.
questions.explanation: The reformer invokes moral law, useful knowledge, public reason, and criticism of harmful customs.

questions.prompt: Originalized letter by an Indian social reformer, c. 1830: “A society cannot call itself enlightened while widows are burned, girls are denied learning, and sacred words are guarded from public reason. We must use both moral law and useful knowledge to reform custom.” The call for “useful knowledge” most closely connects the letter to which nineteenth-century development?

choices.A: The growing prestige of print culture, secular education, and public debate in reform movements
choices.B: The decline of all literacy in colonial societies
choices.C: The replacement of written debate by oral-only political traditions
choices.D: The use of nuclear technology in social reform

questions.correctAnswerText: The growing prestige of print culture, secular education, and public debate in reform movements
questions.explanation: “Useful knowledge” and public reason connect the letter to education and print-based reform debates.

questions.prompt:
Table: Brazil, labor transition in coffee regions, approximate pattern
Period | Enslaved labor | Immigrant wage labor | Political debate
1850s | High | Low | Gradual abolition discussed
1880s | Declining | Rising | Abolitionist pressure increases
1890s | Legally abolished | High | Planters recruit workers from Europe and Japan
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Abolition ended plantation agriculture immediately in Brazil.
choices.B: Coffee planters shifted labor strategies as slavery declined and was abolished.
choices.C: Brazil industrialized before plantation exports developed.
choices.D: Immigrant labor disappeared after abolition.

questions.correctAnswerText: Coffee planters shifted labor strategies as slavery declined and was abolished.
questions.explanation: The table shows enslaved labor declining while immigrant wage labor rises.

questions.prompt:
Table: Brazil, labor transition in coffee regions, approximate pattern
Period | Enslaved labor | Immigrant wage labor | Political debate
1850s | High | Low | Gradual abolition discussed
1880s | Declining | Rising | Abolitionist pressure increases
1890s | Legally abolished | High | Planters recruit workers from Europe and Japan
Which evidence best supports the claim that abolition was connected to changing labor systems?

choices.A: “Planters recruit workers from Europe and Japan”
choices.B: “Gradual abolition discussed”
choices.D: “Political debate”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Planters recruit workers from Europe and Japan”
questions.explanation: This directly shows a new labor strategy after abolition.

questions.prompt:
Table: Brazil, labor transition in coffee regions, approximate pattern
Period | Enslaved labor | Immigrant wage labor | Political debate
1850s | High | Low | Gradual abolition discussed
1880s | Declining | Rising | Abolitionist pressure increases
1890s | Legally abolished | High | Planters recruit workers from Europe and Japan
The legal abolition of slavery in Brazil was part of which broader nineteenth-century development?

choices.A: The expansion of serfdom across the Americas
choices.B: The global decline of coerced chattel slavery under abolitionist pressure
choices.C: The restoration of hereditary monarchy in every Atlantic society
choices.D: The disappearance of export agriculture in Latin America

questions.correctAnswerText: The global decline of coerced chattel slavery under abolitionist pressure
questions.explanation: Brazil’s abolition was part of a wider nineteenth-century abolitionist movement.

questions.prompt:
Table: Brazil, labor transition in coffee regions, approximate pattern
Period | Enslaved labor | Immigrant wage labor | Political debate
1850s | High | Low | Gradual abolition discussed
1880s | Declining | Rising | Abolitionist pressure increases
1890s | Legally abolished | High | Planters recruit workers from Europe and Japan
Which broader social change is most directly suggested by the table?

choices.A: The end of all migration to Latin America
choices.B: The complete equality of formerly enslaved people after abolition
choices.C: The replacement of plantation society by subsistence farming
choices.D: The growth of multiethnic labor forces in some export economies

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of multiethnic labor forces in some export economies
questions.explanation: Recruitment from Europe and Japan suggests increasingly diverse labor forces in Brazilian coffee regions.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of British India c. 1880 shows rail lines radiating from cotton, jute, wheat, and tea districts toward Bombay, Calcutta, Madras, and Karachi. Port symbols mark export docks, while shaded areas show regions linked to plantation or cash-crop production.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Railways helped connect colonial production zones to export ports.
choices.B: British India’s economy became isolated from global markets.
choices.C: Railroads were used mainly to end cash-crop production.
choices.D: Indian ports declined as rail lines expanded.

questions.correctAnswerText: Railways helped connect colonial production zones to export ports.
questions.explanation: The map shows rail lines linking cash-crop regions to major export ports.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of British India c. 1880 shows rail lines radiating from cotton, jute, wheat, and tea districts toward Bombay, Calcutta, Madras, and Karachi. Port symbols mark export docks, while shaded areas show regions linked to plantation or cash-crop production.” The pattern shown most directly reflects which process?

choices.A: The collapse of imperial economic planning
choices.B: Colonial integration into global capitalist markets
choices.C: The replacement of railroads by caravan transport
choices.D: The end of agricultural exports from South Asia

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial integration into global capitalist markets
questions.explanation: Railroads, cash crops, and export ports show colonial integration into global markets.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of British India c. 1880 shows rail lines radiating from cotton, jute, wheat, and tea districts toward Bombay, Calcutta, Madras, and Karachi. Port symbols mark export docks, while shaded areas show regions linked to plantation or cash-crop production.” Which map feature most directly supports the claim that imperial infrastructure reshaped environments for export production?

choices.A: The name “British India”
choices.B: The location of the Indian Ocean
choices.C: The presence of several port cities
choices.D: The shaded cash-crop and plantation regions linked to rail lines

questions.correctAnswerText: The shaded cash-crop and plantation regions linked to rail lines
questions.explanation: These features directly show environmental and economic reorganization for exports.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of British India c. 1880 shows rail lines radiating from cotton, jute, wheat, and tea districts toward Bombay, Calcutta, Madras, and Karachi. Port symbols mark export docks, while shaded areas show regions linked to plantation or cash-crop production.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Railways were politically neutral and unrelated to empire.
choices.B: Colonial governments built infrastructure only for local food security.
choices.C: Industrial-era imperial rule often used infrastructure to strengthen extraction and export.
choices.D: Railroads ended British administrative control in South Asia.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial-era imperial rule often used infrastructure to strengthen extraction and export.
questions.explanation: Railways connected colonial production zones to ports, supporting imperial extraction.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony from East Africa, early twentieth century: “Officials ordered cotton fields planted where food had grown. Men were beaten when the quotas failed. Some elders said the water medicine would turn bullets aside, for the people could no longer endure tax, labor, and forced crops.” What was the most likely purpose of this testimony?

choices.A: To praise colonial agricultural policy as voluntary cooperation
choices.B: To encourage European settlement in New Zealand
choices.C: To describe local grievances and beliefs that contributed to anti-colonial resistance
choices.D: To defend the end of all taxation in European colonies

questions.correctAnswerText: To describe local grievances and beliefs that contributed to anti-colonial resistance
questions.explanation: The testimony highlights forced cotton, violence, taxes, labor, and cultural beliefs tied to resistance.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony from East Africa, early twentieth century: “Officials ordered cotton fields planted where food had grown. Men were beaten when the quotas failed. Some elders said the water medicine would turn bullets aside, for the people could no longer endure tax, labor, and forced crops.” Which statement best evaluates the causes of resistance described?

choices.A: Resistance was caused only by European religious teachings.
choices.B: Resistance resulted from resentment against forced labor, taxation, and coerced cash-crop production.
choices.C: Resistance was unrelated to colonial economic demands.
choices.D: Resistance occurred because local communities wanted more plantation quotas.

questions.correctAnswerText: Resistance resulted from resentment against forced labor, taxation, and coerced cash-crop production.
questions.explanation: The passage directly identifies tax, labor, forced crops, and punishment as grievances.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony from East Africa, early twentieth century: “Officials ordered cotton fields planted where food had grown. Men were beaten when the quotas failed. Some elders said the water medicine would turn bullets aside, for the people could no longer endure tax, labor, and forced crops.” Which phrase best shows that cultural belief shaped resistance?

choices.A: “Water medicine would turn bullets aside”
choices.B: “Cotton fields planted”
choices.C: “Tax, labor, and forced crops”
choices.D: “Officials ordered”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Water medicine would turn bullets aside”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows the role of spiritual belief in resistance.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony from East Africa, early twentieth century: “Officials ordered cotton fields planted where food had grown. Men were beaten when the quotas failed. Some elders said the water medicine would turn bullets aside, for the people could no longer endure tax, labor, and forced crops.” The testimony most directly connects colonial rule to which economic process?

choices.A: The abolition of export agriculture
choices.B: The use of forced production to supply global markets with raw materials
choices.C: The creation of independent African industrial corporations
choices.D: The decline of cash-crop cultivation under imperial rule

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of forced production to supply global markets with raw materials
questions.explanation: Forced cotton cultivation links colonial rule to raw-material production for global markets.

questions.prompt: Described propaganda poster, Japanese-occupied Southeast Asia, c. 1943: “A smiling Japanese soldier points toward a rising sun while farmers from Burma, Malaya, and Indonesia stand beside sacks of rice. The caption says, ‘Asia prospers when Asia is freed from the West.’ Small military aircraft appear in the background.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Japan presented its wartime expansion as anti-Western liberation while using military power to control resources.
choices.B: Japan rejected propaganda as a tool of empire.
choices.C: Southeast Asian farmers controlled Japanese military policy.
choices.D: The poster criticizes Japanese imperialism from an Allied perspective.

questions.correctAnswerText: Japan presented its wartime expansion as anti-Western liberation while using military power to control resources.
questions.explanation: The poster combines anti-Western liberation language with rice resources, soldiers, aircraft, and occupation.

questions.prompt: Described propaganda poster, Japanese-occupied Southeast Asia, c. 1943: “A smiling Japanese soldier points toward a rising sun while farmers from Burma, Malaya, and Indonesia stand beside sacks of rice. The caption says, ‘Asia prospers when Asia is freed from the West.’ Small military aircraft appear in the background.” Which detail most directly suggests the economic importance of occupied territories?

choices.A: The smiling soldier
choices.B: The rising sun
choices.C: The sacks of rice beside Southeast Asian farmers
choices.D: The anti-Western caption

questions.correctAnswerText: The sacks of rice beside Southeast Asian farmers
questions.explanation: The rice sacks indicate the economic importance of agricultural resources in occupied territories.

questions.prompt: Described propaganda poster, Japanese-occupied Southeast Asia, c. 1943: “A smiling Japanese soldier points toward a rising sun while farmers from Burma, Malaya, and Indonesia stand beside sacks of rice. The caption says, ‘Asia prospers when Asia is freed from the West.’ Small military aircraft appear in the background.” Which statement best evaluates the poster’s argument?

choices.A: It openly admits that Japan intended to restore European colonial rule.
choices.B: It denies that military force played any role in wartime Asia.
choices.C: It argues that Southeast Asian independence should be delayed until after industrialization.
choices.D: It uses anti-colonial language to justify Japanese imperial control during World War II.

questions.correctAnswerText: It uses anti-colonial language to justify Japanese imperial control during World War II.
questions.explanation: The caption presents Japan as liberating Asia while the military imagery reveals imperial control.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Korea, 1950–1953, shows rapid movement of front lines from the 38th parallel southward, then northward near the Yalu River, and finally back close to the 38th parallel. Symbols mark United Nations forces, North Korean forces, Chinese intervention, and heavy civilian displacement.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: The Korean War ended all Cold War tensions in East Asia.
choices.B: The conflict remained entirely within North Korea.
choices.C: The Korean War involved international intervention and shifting front lines before ending near its starting boundary.
choices.D: The United Nations refused to participate in the conflict.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Korean War involved international intervention and shifting front lines before ending near its starting boundary.
questions.explanation: The map shows UN forces, Chinese intervention, major front-line shifts, and a final boundary near the 38th parallel.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Korea, 1950–1953, shows rapid movement of front lines from the 38th parallel southward, then northward near the Yalu River, and finally back close to the 38th parallel. Symbols mark United Nations forces, North Korean forces, Chinese intervention, and heavy civilian displacement.” Which social consequence is most directly shown by the map?

choices.A: The disappearance of refugees from twentieth-century wars
choices.B: Large-scale civilian displacement caused by military conflict
choices.C: The complete protection of civilians by Cold War armies
choices.D: The end of urban destruction in modern warfare

questions.correctAnswerText: Large-scale civilian displacement caused by military conflict
questions.explanation: The map’s displacement symbols directly show civilians forced to move by war.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Korea, 1950–1953, shows rapid movement of front lines from the 38th parallel southward, then northward near the Yalu River, and finally back close to the 38th parallel. Symbols mark United Nations forces, North Korean forces, Chinese intervention, and heavy civilian displacement.” Which broader context best explains the conflict shown?

choices.A: Cold War rivalry and the division of Korea into competing political systems
choices.B: The decline of all communist movements after World War I
choices.C: The creation of the Indian Ocean trade network
choices.D: The unification of East Asia under one democratic government

questions.correctAnswerText: Cold War rivalry and the division of Korea into competing political systems
questions.explanation: The conflict grew from Cold War division and competing communist and anti-communist governments.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Korea, 1950–1953, shows rapid movement of front lines from the 38th parallel southward, then northward near the Yalu River, and finally back close to the 38th parallel. Symbols mark United Nations forces, North Korean forces, Chinese intervention, and heavy civilian displacement.” Which feature of the map best illustrates the internationalization of the conflict?

choices.A: The Korean peninsula’s coastline
choices.B: The position of the 38th parallel
choices.C: The location of rivers and mountains
choices.D: The symbols marking United Nations forces and Chinese intervention

questions.correctAnswerText: The symbols marking United Nations forces and Chinese intervention
questions.explanation: Those symbols show that the conflict involved outside powers, not only Korean forces.

questions.prompt: Originalized refugee agency report, late 1970s: “Families fleeing war in Indochina crowd boats and camps; others cross mountains from Afghanistan or leave drought-stricken areas in the Horn of Africa. Relief workers ask governments for resettlement places, food, and legal protection.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To mobilize international assistance and legal protection for displaced populations
choices.B: To encourage states to abolish all refugee laws
choices.C: To prove that migration ended after decolonization
choices.D: To defend colonial rule in Asia and Africa

questions.correctAnswerText: To mobilize international assistance and legal protection for displaced populations
questions.explanation: The report asks governments for resettlement, food, and legal protection.

questions.prompt: Originalized refugee agency report, late 1970s: “Families fleeing war in Indochina crowd boats and camps; others cross mountains from Afghanistan or leave drought-stricken areas in the Horn of Africa. Relief workers ask governments for resettlement places, food, and legal protection.” Which phrase best supports the claim that displacement had multiple causes?

choices.A: “Crowd boats and camps”
choices.B: “Relief workers”
choices.C: “War in Indochina,” “Afghanistan,” and “drought-stricken areas”
choices.D: “Legal protection”

questions.correctAnswerText: “War in Indochina,” “Afghanistan,” and “drought-stricken areas”
questions.explanation: These examples show that displacement came from war, invasion, and environmental stress.

questions.prompt: Originalized refugee agency report, late 1970s: “Families fleeing war in Indochina crowd boats and camps; others cross mountains from Afghanistan or leave drought-stricken areas in the Horn of Africa. Relief workers ask governments for resettlement places, food, and legal protection.” The report most directly reflects which post-1945 development?

choices.A: The disappearance of international institutions
choices.B: The growth of global humanitarian and refugee-protection systems
choices.C: The end of war-related migration
choices.D: The return of mandate systems after World War II

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of global humanitarian and refugee-protection systems
questions.explanation: The report reflects post-1945 institutions and norms for refugees and displaced people.

questions.prompt: Originalized refugee agency report, late 1970s: “Families fleeing war in Indochina crowd boats and camps; others cross mountains from Afghanistan or leave drought-stricken areas in the Horn of Africa. Relief workers ask governments for resettlement places, food, and legal protection.” Which later globalization-era issue shows continuity with the report?

choices.A: The end of cross-border migration after the Cold War
choices.B: The decline of humanitarian organizations after 1945
choices.C: The disappearance of legal debates over asylum
choices.D: Ongoing international debates over refugees, borders, and humanitarian responsibility

questions.correctAnswerText: Ongoing international debates over refugees, borders, and humanitarian responsibility
questions.explanation: Modern globalization continues to involve debates over refugee protection, migration, and state borders.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global consumption of ozone-depleting CFCs, simplified index
Year | Global CFC consumption index, 1986 = 100
1986 | 100
1995 | 42
2010 | 3
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: CFC use increased steadily after 1986.
choices.B: Environmental regulation had no effect on industrial chemicals.
choices.C: International efforts helped sharply reduce CFC consumption.
choices.D: CFCs were used only before World War II.

questions.correctAnswerText: International efforts helped sharply reduce CFC consumption.
questions.explanation: The index falls from 100 to 3, consistent with successful global regulation of ozone-depleting chemicals.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global consumption of ozone-depleting CFCs, simplified index
Year | Global CFC consumption index, 1986 = 100
1986 | 100
1995 | 42
2010 | 3
Which purpose would international environmental agreements most likely have had in relation to the trend shown?

choices.A: To increase CFC production for export
choices.B: To coordinate state action to phase out chemicals damaging the ozone layer
choices.C: To end all scientific research on the atmosphere
choices.D: To transfer environmental authority to colonial empires

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate state action to phase out chemicals damaging the ozone layer
questions.explanation: Environmental agreements sought coordinated action against chemicals that harmed the ozone layer.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global consumption of ozone-depleting CFCs, simplified index
Year | Global CFC consumption index, 1986 = 100
1986 | 100
1995 | 42
2010 | 3
Which evidence best supports the claim that consumption declined dramatically?

choices.A: The index fell from 100 in 1986 to 3 in 2010.
choices.B: The index begins in 1986.
choices.C: The table uses index values.
choices.D: The year 1995 appears between 1986 and 2010.

questions.correctAnswerText: The index fell from 100 in 1986 to 3 in 2010.
questions.explanation: The large decline in the index directly supports the claim of dramatic reduction.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global consumption of ozone-depleting CFCs, simplified index
Year | Global CFC consumption index, 1986 = 100
1986 | 100
1995 | 42
2010 | 3
The trend in the table most directly illustrates which broader feature of globalization?

choices.A: Environmental problems became entirely local after 1945.
choices.B: Global cooperation eliminated all disagreements between rich and poor countries.
choices.C: Scientific knowledge ceased to influence public policy.
choices.D: Transnational scientific and political cooperation could address shared environmental risks.

questions.correctAnswerText: Transnational scientific and political cooperation could address shared environmental risks.
questions.explanation: The trend reflects global cooperation based on scientific evidence and international policy.
